inspector_notes: Observed a new instance of surface delamination on the structural-panel, specifically on the upper third of the south-facing exterior surface, approximately 1.5 meters from the eastern edge. The affected area measures approximately 5cm x 3cm, showing distinct flaking of the protective coating. This appears to be a new finding since the last inspection conducted on 2024-06-15, where no such anomaly was recorded. While currently assessed as low severity, this condition could potentially expose the substrate to environmental factors, possibly leading to accelerated corrosion if left unaddressed. Recommend a follow-up close-range visual inspection by ground personnel to precisely determine the depth of delamination and schedule localized repair to restore coating integrity.

During the routine flight, the main compressor unit on the eastern side of HVAC Unit #3 displayed a subtle, rhythmic lateral vibration, estimated at 1-2mm displacement, specifically around the south-east mounting point. The acoustic sensor also detected a new, low-frequency hum emanating from the unit, which was not present in the last inspection report dated 2024-03-15, indicating this is a new anomaly. Although the current severity is low, unchecked vibration can accelerate wear on internal components, loosen fasteners, and lead to premature mechanical failure, posing a long-term operational risk and potential compliance issues related to equipment longevity. A manual technician inspection is recommended to diagnose the root cause and ensure the structural integrity of the compressor unit, followed by a re-evaluation during the next scheduled drone flight.

Observed a distinct circumferential crack and partial breach on a 2-inch PVC electrical conduit, measuring approximately 15mm in length and 3mm wide, with visible wire insulation beneath. This anomaly is situated on the underside of the main feeder conduit running parallel to the north-facing wall, roughly 2.5 meters west of junction box A-4 within Zone-A. This conduit breach was not documented during the previous inspection on 2024-04-15 and is considered a new finding. The open breach presents a direct risk of moisture ingress and potential short-circuiting, indicating a medium severity compliance violation. Immediate manual inspection by a certified electrician is recommended to assess internal wire integrity and schedule prompt repair or replacement of the affected segment.

Observed a distinct spiderweb-pattern crack across a single solar cell, approximately 7cm in diameter, located on the third panel from the east within array string B, top row. This damage was not observed in previous inspection data from 2024-03-01, indicating it is a new development. The cracked cell significantly reduces panel efficiency and presents a medium risk for hot spot development, potentially impacting overall array performance and longevity. Recommend scheduling a ground-level thermal inspection of panel G12 to assess hot spot risk and determine the optimal replacement strategy.

Drone imagery confirms three critical fasteners are missing from the upper-left mounting bracket of structural-panel SP-Z01-03 in Zone-A, located on the north-facing elevation. This anomaly was not observed or documented during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-06-15, indicating a new or significantly worsened condition. The absence of these fasteners compromises the structural integrity of the panel, especially under potential wind loading, posing a significant safety hazard and a compliance breach for structural stability. Immediate ground-level inspection by a qualified structural engineer is recommended, along with the urgent replacement of all compromised fasteners and a full assessment of the panel's attachment points.

Approximately 3 cm of red insulated wiring observed exposed at a flex joint on the primary electrical-conduit run, specifically at the junction box just east of the Zone-A main power feed. The insulation appears chafed rather than cleanly severed, with no immediate sign of conductor fraying. This exposed section was not present in the 2024-11-15 inspection data and appears to be a new development. It poses a potential low-voltage short circuit risk and constitutes a minor NEC compliance deviation. Recommend a ground-level visual inspection by a qualified electrician during the next scheduled maintenance window to assess the extent of insulation damage and apply appropriate protective measures.

Thermal imaging revealed a severe hotspot exceeding 85°C (ambient 32°C) emanating from the rear junction box of a single solar module, indicative of a critical connection failure or arc fault. This specific anomaly is located on Panel 14 in Row 3 of the north-facing array section, connected to inverter string 2. No similar thermal signatures were present at this location during the last inspection conducted on 2024-05-15, identifying this as a new and escalating fault. The elevated temperature poses a significant fire risk to the surrounding modules and cabling, and represents a direct breach of operational safety protocols due to potential arc flash. Immediate isolation of inverter string 2 is recommended, followed by a detailed ground-based inspection and repair by qualified personnel.

Thermal imaging revealed a distinct hotspot on solar panel SN: AUST-ZA-P14, registering 78.3°C, significantly exceeding adjacent panel temperatures by over 30°C. The anomaly is located on the lower-right quadrant of the panel, situated in the third row from the east within Zone-A's east-facing array. This hotspot was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-11-15, indicating a new fault development. The elevated temperature presents a significant fire risk and indicates potential cell degradation, impacting the array's overall power output and operational safety. Immediate investigation by ground crew is recommended to determine the root cause, followed by isolation and potential replacement of the affected panel.

Observed significant accumulation of fine silt and organic matter within the drainage system, specifically measuring approximately 15-20mm deep along the westernmost branch of the main storm drain line in Zone-B, about 50m downstream from the intake grate. This sediment buildup was not noted during the last inspection on 2024-02-15 and represents a new finding for this specific section. While currently a LOW severity, continued accumulation will reduce flow capacity, potentially leading to blockages and localized standing water, impacting site drainage compliance. Recommend scheduling a routine flushing or manual removal of the sediment within the next 3-6 months to prevent further accumulation and maintain optimal system performance. Further drone monitoring during future inspections is advised to track sediment levels in adjacent sections.

Observed a distinct thermal hotspot on solar panel #B-14-07, located in the third row from the western edge of Zone-B. Thermal imaging indicates a surface temperature of 78°C, significantly higher than adjacent panels (avg. 45°C) under similar insolation. This hotspot was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-03-15 and appears to be a new development. A persistent hotspot of this magnitude can indicate a failing cell or bypass diode, posing a risk of reduced power output and potential fire hazard if it escalates, compromising system efficiency and long-term asset integrity. Recommend a ground-based electrical inspection using an IV curve tracer and detailed visual check to determine the root cause. Further monitoring via thermal imaging is advised following any intervention.

A small, localized dent approximately 5cm in diameter with minor paint chipping was observed on the lower western edge of structural-panel B-07, near ground level. This appears to be a new finding since the previous inspection on 2024-09-01, where no damage was recorded in this area. Given its small size and location on a non-load-bearing section, the immediate structural integrity risk is minimal. However, the exposed metal could lead to surface corrosion over time, potentially compromising the panel's protective coating and long-term durability. A localized repair and paint touch-up is recommended to prevent further degradation and maintain compliance with surface finish standards. The area should be re-inspected during the next scheduled flight to monitor for any changes.

Drone observed approximately 3-4 inches of red insulation stripped from a primary conductor within the electrical conduit, revealing exposed copper wiring that appears slightly corroded. This anomaly is located on the underside of the main feeder conduit entering the Zone-B sub-panel, approximately 1.5 meters from the panel housing. This specific instance of exposed wiring was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2023-10-15, indicating a new defect. The exposed conductor presents a high risk of short-circuit, arc flash, and potential electrocution, significantly compromising electrical safety and compliance with NEC standards. Immediate investigation and repair by qualified electrical personnel are recommended to mitigate the hazard, with power isolation advised before close inspection.

Observed a significant pipe separation anomaly within the primary stormwater drainage line, characterized by an approximate 1.5-inch gap between two concrete segments. Evidence of active water egress and localized soil saturation is clearly visible. This separation is located on segment D-12, approximately 20 feet downstream from pump station 3, on the west bank of Zone-B. This specific separation appears to be a new finding, as no comparable anomaly was detected or recorded during the previous comprehensive inspection conducted on 2024-08-20. The observed separation presents a high risk of environmental contamination due to potential untreated discharge and compromises the overall integrity and function of the drainage system, posing a compliance violation. Immediate on-site investigation by ground crew is recommended to assess the structural integrity and determine the root cause, followed by an urgent repair plan.

Observed a distinct thermal hotspot on solar panel B-12, exhibiting a peak temperature of 85.3°C, appearing as a concentrated bright area in the IR spectrum approximately 15x15 cm. This hotspot is located near the bottom-left corner of the module within the Zone-B solar array. Comparison with the previous inspection data from 2024-02-15 indicates this hotspot is a new finding; it was not present or detectable during the last survey. The elevated temperature suggests a potential cell defect or micro-crack, which could lead to reduced energy output and accelerated degradation, posing a medium-term operational efficiency risk. Recommend a localized ground inspection with an advanced thermal camera and electrical testing to diagnose the root cause and determine if panel replacement is required.

A prominent arc mark and visibly dislodged DC connector plug were observed at the inter-panel wiring connection between solar panels B12 and B13, measuring approximately 3cm in length. This anomaly is specifically located on the underside of the central string's fourth panel (B12) on the north-facing edge, near its junction box. This connection failure was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-05-15, indicating a new development. The visible arcing poses a significant fire hazard and indicates complete loss of output from the affected string, impacting overall array efficiency and site safety. Immediate ground-based investigation and a qualified electrician's intervention are recommended to assess the damage, isolate the affected string, and perform necessary repairs or component replacement.

Observed significant sediment buildup within the main outflow channel of the Zone-B drainage system, estimated to reduce flow capacity by 40-50% along the lower invert. This accumulation is concentrated directly downstream from the primary storm grate access point in Section B-3, extending approximately 5 meters into the main pipe run. This represents a significant worsening since the last inspection on 2024-03-15, where only minor, easily flushed sediment was noted, reducing flow by less than 10%. The severe reduction in drainage capacity poses a high risk of localized flooding during heavy rainfall events, potentially impacting adjacent critical infrastructure and creating compliance issues for stormwater management regulations. Immediate physical inspection and hydro-jetting operations are recommended to restore full drainage capacity. Further investigation into the source of accelerated sediment deposition is also advised.

Observed significant charring and a partially dislodged DC input cable at Junction Box JBC-04, specifically connecting to solar panel R3-P7. The damaged section spans approximately 5cm of insulation, with clear evidence of arc flash marks around the terminal. This connection failure is a new finding, as no anomalies were recorded at this specific junction during the previous inspection on 2024-11-15. The observed damage poses a high risk of electrical fire and compromises system integrity, requiring urgent attention to prevent further safety hazards and maintain compliance. Immediate on-site investigation by qualified personnel, isolation of the affected string, and full replacement of the damaged cable and connector are strongly recommended.

Observed significant oily residue and localized frost buildup (approx. 5cm diameter) around the flare connection of the liquid line on the north-facing side of the primary compressor unit. This is a new finding; no such indications were present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-03-15. The high severity refrigerant leak presents a critical environmental compliance issue and will result in rapidly deteriorating cooling performance and potential compressor failure if not addressed promptly. Recommend immediate system shutdown and dispatch of a certified HVAC technician for urgent leak detection, repair, and refrigerant recovery/recharge.

Thermal imaging captured a severe hotspot on the 2-inch diameter electrical conduit located approximately 1.5 meters from the main junction box on the west wall of Zone-C. The conduit surface temperature registered 78.3°C, with visible evidence of insulation discoloration and minor blistering. This specific overheating anomaly is a new finding, as no thermal irregularities were detected at this location during the previous inspection on 2023-04-12. The high temperature poses a significant fire risk and potential for electrical failure, indicating a serious violation of electrical safety standards. Immediate investigation by a certified electrician is highly recommended, potentially requiring a localized circuit shutdown to ensure safety during assessment and repair.

Thermal imaging on solar array panel C-17, row 5, revealed a hot spot consistent with delamination and significant cell damage affecting approximately 15% of the panel's surface area. This anomaly was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2023-11-20, indicating a new occurrence within the last 12 months. The localized overheating poses a moderate risk of further panel degradation and potential energy yield reduction for this section of the array. Continued operation with this level of damage may lead to reduced overall efficiency for Zone-C. It is recommended that a ground crew conduct a physical inspection of panel C-17 to assess the extent of the damage and confirm the root cause. Replacement or repair should be scheduled promptly to mitigate further losses.

Observed a fresh impact gouge on structural panel SP-C-05, measuring approximately 15 cm in length, 2 cm wide, and an estimated 0.5 cm deep at its deepest point, exposing the underlying primer layer. This damage is located on the lower-left quadrant of structural panel SP-C-05, approximately 1.5 meters from the ground, facing west within Zone-C. This physical damage was not present during the last scheduled inspection on 2024-12-15, indicating a new finding. The depth of the gouge suggests potential compromise to the panel's protective coating and possibly localized structural integrity, increasing the risk of corrosion and potential fatigue over time if unaddressed. Recommend a ground-level manual inspection by maintenance personnel for further assessment of depth and potential structural compromise, followed by immediate repair to prevent further deterioration.

A visible separation, approximately 4mm wide, was identified between two 300mm PVC pipe segments within the main drainage line, located about 15 meters downstream from the Zone-C primary inlet. This gap was not present in the reference imagery from the previous inspection on 2024-05-10, indicating a new development. While no active leakage or significant ground saturation was observed, this separation poses a potential risk for future water egress, localized erosion, and could compromise the long-term integrity of the drainage system if unaddressed. Recommend scheduling a manual ground-level inspection by maintenance personnel to further evaluate the joint and determine appropriate remediation steps, such as re-sealing or coupling repair.

Observed a new hairline crack, approximately 4.5 cm in length, located near the lower-left corner of structural-panel C-03 on its eastern face within Zone-C. This appears to be a new anomaly since the previous inspection on 2024-03-15, where no damage was noted in this specific area. While the crack currently seems superficial and presents a low structural risk, it could worsen with freeze-thaw cycles or impact future panel integrity. Compliance is not immediately impacted, but continued propagation could eventually lead to water ingress. Recommend a follow-up ground-based visual verification to confirm its depth and assess if a sealant application or minor patching is required.

Observed a distinct oily residue approximately 5cm in diameter with associated minor frost accumulation around the flared connection of the liquid line service valve. This anomaly is located on the west-facing side of the primary condenser unit within Zone-C of the rooftop-hvac system. This refrigerant leak indication was not present during the last scheduled inspection on 2024-11-15, marking it as a new finding. Such a leak represents a direct environmental compliance violation due to refrigerant release and will lead to significant degradation of system efficiency and potential compressor damage. Recommend immediate dispatch of an HVAC technician for manual inspection, leak detection, repair, and refrigerant recovery/recharge.

A ~15 cm section of the primary electrical conduit's outer insulation is visibly frayed and torn, exposing the internal conductor sheathing. This damage is located on the south-facing run of the conduit where it enters the main switchgear cabinet in Zone-C, approximately 1.5 meters from the floor. This specific point of insulation degradation was not noted during the previous inspection on 2024-08-10, indicating a new anomaly. The exposed sheathing presents a potential risk for moisture ingress and accelerated material degradation, which could lead to a short circuit or ground fault, violating electrical safety standards. Recommend a priority ground-level assessment by a certified electrician to determine the extent of conductor exposure and schedule immediate repair or replacement of the affected conduit section.

Observed significant debris accumulation, including dense leaf matter, plastic sheeting, and visible bird nesting material, covering approximately 40% of the south-facing exterior condenser coil on the rooftop HVAC unit in Zone-C. This blockage is a new finding since the previous inspection on 2024-04-20, indicating a rapid degradation of coil condition. Such extensive obstruction will severely restrict airflow, leading to reduced cooling efficiency, increased energy consumption, and a significant risk of compressor overheating and premature failure. This poses a compliance issue regarding operational efficiency and equipment longevity. Recommend immediate manual cleaning of the condenser coil by a qualified maintenance team, followed by a post-cleaning thermal scan to confirm restored airflow and optimal operating temperatures.

A new diagonal crack, approximately 15 cm long and 2-3 mm wide, with minor surface flaking, was observed on structural-panel C-4. The anomaly is located on the lower-right section of the panel, roughly 1.5 meters from the base. This damage was not present during the previous inspection on 2024-05-15, marking it as a new finding. The crack could indicate underlying stress and, if left unaddressed, may compromise the panel's long-term structural integrity and compliance. A follow-up human inspection by a structural engineer is recommended to assess crack depth and prescribe appropriate repair or monitoring actions.

Detected significant abnormal vibration originating from the primary compressor unit on HVAC Unit R-03, situated on the north-east corner of the rooftop equipment pad; the amplitude of vibration caused a visible shudder in connected refrigerant lines. This high-severity finding is new and was not observed during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-11-15. The excessive vibration poses an immediate risk of mechanical failure to the compressor and could lead to refrigerant leaks, creating a safety hazard and potential environmental compliance violation. Continued operation under these conditions will likely result in a complete system shutdown and costly repairs. Urgent dispatch of a qualified HVAC technician is recommended to diagnose the root cause, mitigate the risk of catastrophic failure, and restore safe operation.

Observed a small, iridescent oily residue stain, approximately 3cm in diameter, consistent with a minor refrigerant leak. This anomaly is located at the brazed joint of the liquid line connection on the north-eastern compressor unit of the rooftop-hvac system. This appears to be a new finding since the last inspection flight on 2023-12-09. While the leak is currently low severity, it poses a minor environmental compliance risk and will lead to decreased operational efficiency over time. Recommend a follow-up manual inspection by an HVAC technician to precisely locate and repair the source of the leak.

Observed severe, non-uniform soiling patterns across approximately 15% of Zone-C panels, specifically rows 3-5, characterized by heavy, dried mud streaks on the lower third of south-facing modules. This accumulation significantly reduces light transmittance by an estimated 40% in affected areas. This condition represents a significant worsening since the 2024-10-15 inspection, which only noted minor dust. The extensive soiling risks substantial power generation efficiency loss and potential long-term panel degradation via hot spots, impacting overall site output compliance. Immediate manual cleaning of all affected panels in Zone-C rows 3-5 is critical, and a root cause analysis for the heavy soiling source is recommended to prevent future recurrence.

A distinct thermal hotspot, approximately 15x15 cm with a temperature of +67°C (+22°C above adjacent panels), was identified on Panel 3B-7 within Zone-D of the solar array, specifically on the bottom-right cell of the south-facing string. This anomaly was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-04-15 and represents a new finding. Sustained elevated temperatures could indicate cell degradation, potentially leading to reduced energy output and premature panel failure. If left unaddressed, this poses a risk of localized fire and impacts overall site efficiency, potentially affecting compliance targets. A ground-level inspection and electrical performance testing of Panel 3B-7 is recommended within 48 hours to diagnose the root cause and facilitate timely repair.

Significant, oscillating vibration was observed on the southernmost compressor unit (Unit #3) within the main rooftop HVAC enclosure in Zone-D. Visual analysis of high-resolution stills shows slight blurring of peripheral components and notable movement exceeding normal operational parameters. This excessive vibration is a new finding, as no similar anomaly was recorded during the previous inspection on 2024-03-15. Such sustained vibration poses a medium severity risk of premature component wear, potential mounting failure, and could lead to refrigerant leakage or complete unit shutdown, impacting site operations and environmental compliance. Immediate ground team follow-up is recommended to assess the vibration source and initiate corrective maintenance procedures.

Thermal imaging identified a distinct hotspot on solar panel D-S-14, exhibiting a peak temperature of 85°C, spanning approximately 15x15cm, significantly above the average panel temperature of 38°C. This anomaly is located on the lower-right quadrant of panel D-S-14, which is situated on the third row from the western edge of Zone-D within the solar-array. This specific thermal signature was not present during the previous inspection on 2024-02-15, indicating a new and concerning development. The high temperature differential suggests a severe electrical fault, posing a significant fire risk and immediate threat to energy production compliance. Immediate ground-level inspection by a qualified electrical technician is required to diagnose the root cause and schedule repair or replacement of the affected panel.

Observed a moderate accumulation of fine, silty sediment, approximately 5-7mm deep, along the bottom of the main outflow channel within Zone-D's drainage system, specifically at the bend approaching the retention pond. This represents a new finding since the last inspection on 2024-09-15, where no significant sediment was present in this section. While currently a low severity anomaly, sustained buildup could progressively reduce flow capacity, potentially impacting stormwater management compliance during heavy precipitation. Recommend scheduling a follow-up visual inspection within 3 months to monitor the accumulation rate and consider localized flushing or manual removal if the buildup accelerates.

Observed a new instance of surface delamination on structural Panel SP-D4, located in the top-right quadrant, approximately 1.5m from the north edge. The delaminated area measures roughly 5x3 cm and appears as a raised bubble with minor flaking at the edges. This anomaly was not present in the previous inspection report from 2024-09-20, indicating a new development. While currently low severity, it suggests potential for moisture intrusion and could progress, compromising the panel's protective coating. Recommend a follow-up physical inspection by maintenance personnel during the next scheduled shutdown to assess the extent and determine if minor surface repair is warranted.

A small, dime-sized oily residue with trace bubbling was observed on the main refrigerant line connection entering the compressor unit. This anomaly is situated on the north-facing side of the rooftop-hvac unit's compressor bay. This finding was not present during the previous inspection on 2024-08-15 and appears to be a new development. While currently low severity, it indicates minor refrigerant loss which can impact efficiency and poses a slight environmental concern. Recommend a ground-level technician inspection to pinpoint the leak source and assess the integrity of the connection point for repair.

Observed a significant blockage within the main subsurface drainage pipe of Zone-D, approximately 15 meters downstream from access point D4. The pipe appears to be 60-70% constricted by a dense accumulation of organic debris, including leaves and small branches, mixed with fine sediment. This specific blockage was not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-03-15, indicating a rapid development of the issue. Such an obstruction poses a medium-term risk of reduced drainage efficiency, potential localized overflow, and could lead to system back pressure or environmental discharge non-compliance. Immediate dispatch of ground crew is recommended for further investigation and physical clearing to restore full flow and prevent escalation.

During the 2024-10-14 flight, three critical structural fasteners were observed missing from the bottom row of structural panel D-12, located on the southwest corner of the main building in Zone-D. The empty bolt holes exhibit minor rust, suggesting the fasteners have been absent for some time. This specific anomaly was not documented in the previous inspection report from 2023-11-01, indicating a new or significantly worsened condition since the last assessment. The absence of these fasteners severely compromises the structural integrity of panel D-12, presenting a high risk of localized failure and potential for panel detachment, especially during adverse weather. Immediate ground-based verification by a structural engineer is recommended, followed by urgent scheduling for full fastener replacement and a review of the panel's remaining service life.

Observed a new instance of surface delamination on structural panel SP-14, specifically on the upper-left quadrant of its north-facing side, approximately 15 meters above ground level. The anomaly measures roughly 7 cm x 4 cm and exhibits slight uplifting of the outer composite layer, exposing the underlying material. This specific delamination was not present in the last inspection report dated 2024-03-15, indicating a new development. While currently assessed as low severity, this type of degradation can compromise weatherproofing and could signal early material fatigue, potentially affecting long-term structural integrity if left unaddressed. Recommend a follow-up visual inspection by ground personnel to assess depth and extent, and to determine the appropriate repair strategy for material preservation.

A small, oily residue, approximately 5cm in diameter, was observed on the suction line connection at the condenser unit, accompanied by slight discoloration on the adjacent insulation wrap. This anomaly is located on the north-east corner condenser unit of the rooftop-hvac system in Zone-D. This appears to be a new finding, as no residue or discoloration was noted in the previous inspection report. While currently low severity, a continuous refrigerant leak can lead to reduced HVAC efficiency and minor environmental impact. Recommend a ground-based technician inspect the connection for sealant integrity and pressure levels, and address the leak promptly.

A localized breach, approximately 5mm in diameter, was observed on the underside of the main feeder electrical conduit running along the eastern wall of Zone-D, roughly 2.5 meters above ground level, adjacent to breaker panel 3A. The breach exposes internal wiring and shows minor corrosion around its edges. This conduit breach is a new finding, not present during the previous inspection conducted on 2024-04-15. The exposed wiring presents a potential electrical hazard due to moisture ingress or physical contact, posing a clear compliance violation. Recommend a ground team conduct an immediate manual inspection to fully assess the extent of the damage and schedule prompt repair or replacement of the compromised section.

Observed a distinct thermal hotspot measuring approximately 15x20 cm on the main condenser coil of rooftop HVAC Unit D-3, located on the north-eastern side within Zone-D. The affected area registered a surface temperature of 35°C above the average ambient unit temperature, indicating significant localized heat generation. This anomaly is a new finding and was not present in the thermal imaging data from the previous inspection conducted on 2024-07-15. Sustained elevated temperatures like this suggest potential component stress, restricted airflow, or early-stage electrical fault, which could lead to reduced efficiency, premature equipment failure, and poses a fire risk if left unaddressed. Recommend immediate manual inspection by an HVAC technician, including contact thermography and electrical checks, to identify the root cause and implement corrective maintenance actions promptly.

A visible separation was observed between two sections of 150mm PVC pipe, displaying a uniform gap of approximately 5-7mm along the pipe's circumference. This anomaly is located at the junction where Lateral C connects to the main drainage line, roughly 12 meters downstream from access point E-03 within Zone-E's drainage-system. This separation was not noted in the previous inspection report dated 2024-12-01, indicating a new development. While currently a low severity, this condition could progressively lead to minor sub-surface leakage, potential for root intrusion, and reduced flow efficiency over time if not addressed. Recommend scheduling a manual ground inspection with a plumber for a closer assessment and to plan for appropriate sealant application or minor re-coupling during the next maintenance cycle.

Observed a distinct separation of approximately 15-20mm at the bell-and-spigot joint of the 8-inch PVC drainage pipe, with minor soil infiltration visible within the gap. This anomaly is located on the downstream side of manhole E-4, specifically where the tertiary line from pump station 2 connects to the main Zone-E collector line. This separation was not present during the last inspection on 2024-04-15 and appears to be a new development. The separation presents a risk of localized leaks, potential soil erosion around the pipe, and reduced hydraulic efficiency, potentially leading to blockages or structural compromise if left unaddressed. Recommend a follow-up ground-level inspection by maintenance personnel to confirm the extent of the separation and assess structural integrity, followed by scheduling for repair.

A section of insulation damage was observed on the electrical-conduit, manifesting as a jagged tear approximately 3cm in length, exposing the underlying conductor shielding. This anomaly is located on the upper surface of the 2-inch diameter main trunk line, approximately 1.5 meters east of the Zone-E primary junction box. This specific damage was not noted during the previous inspection on 2024-11-15, indicating a new finding requiring attention. The exposed conductor presents a medium safety risk for arc fault or electrocution, also indicating a compliance breach with electrical safety codes. Immediate ground team follow-up is recommended to assess the extent of conductor exposure and schedule necessary repair.

A distinct thermal hotspot measuring approximately 15x15cm with a peak surface temperature of 95°C was identified on the compressor housing of HVAC Unit 3-E, located in Zone-E. This represents a significant delta of +35°C compared to adjacent operational surfaces and was not observed during the previous inspection on 2024-07-15. The elevated temperature suggests potential internal component friction or failure within the compressor. This condition poses a medium risk for premature unit failure, increased energy consumption, and potential safety concerns if left unaddressed. Immediate physical inspection and diagnostic testing by a qualified HVAC technician are recommended to determine the root cause and implement corrective actions.

Observed a complete separation of the DC positive terminal connector on PV panel #14 in string 3, Zone-E, resulting in an open circuit. Significant arcing damage and charring are visible on both the connector housing and adjacent panel frame, measuring approximately 5cm in diameter. This fault is located on the north-easternmost panel of the third row from the main inverter housing within Array Block 4. This connection failure is a new finding; no anomalies were present at this specific point during the previous inspection dated 2024-12-15. The severity of the arcing indicates a significant fire risk and constitutes a critical safety hazard, directly violating electrical safety protocols. Immediate isolation and shutdown of Array Block 4 is recommended, followed by on-site manual inspection and emergency repair by a certified electrician to prevent further damage or incident.

Dense accumulation of organic debris, predominantly leaves and fine particulate, observed causing an estimated 70-75% obstruction across the lower two-thirds of the south-facing condenser coil bank on rooftop HVAC Unit #3. This blockage is a significant new finding and a marked deterioration since the last inspection on 2024-11-15, which showed no such obstruction. The severe impedance to airflow and heat exchange poses a high risk of compressor overheating, reduced efficiency, and potential premature unit failure, thus violating operational performance and safety standards. Immediate manual ground inspection and professional coil cleaning are strongly recommended to mitigate risk and restore proper unit function.

Observed a minor breach on the exterior sheathing of a 2-inch diameter electrical conduit, approximately 1.5 cm in length, revealing the insulation layer beneath. This anomaly is located on the underside of a conduit run connecting substation panel P-03 to junction box J-12, approximately 3 meters east of the P-03 panel within Zone-E. This breach was not present in the previous inspection report from 2024-03-15 and appears to be a new finding. While currently low severity, the exposed insulation could degrade further or allow moisture ingress, potentially leading to future short circuits or compliance violations under NEC article 300.6. Recommend a follow-up visual inspection by ground personnel for a closer assessment and potential repair using appropriate conduit sealing methods to prevent further degradation.

Thermal imaging identified a localized hot spot on the electrical conduit, reaching approximately 85°C (ambient 30°C), with minor charring visible on the insulation surface. This anomaly is situated on the main trunking conduit supplying power to Zone-E, specifically 3 meters west of junction box E-04, on the underside. This condition was not present during the last inspection on 2024-06-15, indicating a new and concerning development. The elevated temperature poses a significant fire risk and suggests potential electrical overload or insulation degradation, violating safety standards. Recommend immediate manual inspection by a qualified electrician to determine the root cause and implement corrective action.
